summary: Gruber systematically dismantles Duncan Riley's attempt to save face after his widely-cited prediction of an '$800 Apple laptop' turned out to be completely wrong. Riley's source had apparently seen a price list but misidentified a new monitor as a laptop and got the price wrong by $100. Gruber translates each of Riley's weasel-worded excuses into plain English, exposing the self-serving logic behind each rationalization. The post highlights how tech bloggers dress up unsubstantiated tips as reliable sourcing to maximize attention and page views, then minimize accountability when proven wrong. Gruber's core complaint is that Riley presented a guess as established fact, then refused to acknowledge the fundamental dishonesty of that approach.
key_points: Riley's original report was entirely wrong — wrong product category and wrong price — yet his 'correction' tries to minimize the errors as minor
The distinction between $800 and $899 is not 'semantics' but a hundred dollars, and any tech blogger should know Apple prices end in 99
Riley described his source as 'reliable' and 'accurate in the past' rather than 'unsubstantiated' because honest framing would have gotten fewer links
Riley's defense that 'someone else would have published it' reveals the real motivation was attention and page views, not journalism
The post illustrates Gruber's broader critique of tech blogging's accountability problem: publish rumors as facts, then treat being wrong as an acceptable cost of doing business
money_quotes: The entire report was completely wrong.
The difference between $899 and $900 is semantics. The difference between $899 and $800 is a hundred dollars.
If our source saw '899' and didn't 'get' the '99' part, it suggests our source is either visually or mentally handicapped.
I reported a guess as a fact.

Sometimes in this game you print unsubstantiated tips, but you describe them like this: 'The information comes from a source we would categorize as reliable,' because if you described them as 'unsubstantiated' or 'sketchily sourced', far fewer people would believe it.
I will continue to publish bogus reports from the same source.
If I just keep digging, surely I'll eventually get out of this hole.
If I hadn't completely trashed my own credibility, someone else would have trashed theirs, and they would have gotten all the attention and page views that instead went to me.
key_insight: Tech bloggers who present unsubstantiated rumors as reliable sourcing to maximize page views forfeit their credibility when wrong, and their post-hoc rationalizations only dig the hole deeper.
